title: The Parent Honouring Ritual
subtitle: A short ritual for marking what a parent gave you and carrying it forward
excerpt: A short ritual for honouring a parent who has died. Works through a release of the unfinished grief, an allow for what they gave you to stay present, and an anchor for carrying them forward. No equipment needed.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. The five-part ritual structure draws on the ceremony shape from MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use on a parent's birthday, the anniversary of their death, or any day when they are particularly present in your thoughts.

content.text: A ten-minute ritual for honouring a parent who has died. The practice holds what they gave you, acknowledges what the loss has meant, and anchors a way of carrying them forward.

Sit somewhere quiet. Hold in mind one specific thing your parent gave you: a quality they modelled, a belief they passed on, a particular memory, a skill. It doesn't have to be positive. It can be something they gave you by way of contrast, something that made you decide to do it differently. Hold it in mind before you begin.
I am ready to release the unfinished grief I am still carrying for them. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow what they gave me to stay with me, in whatever form it takes. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.

Spend two minutes in silence holding the specific thing you identified in the preparation. Let it be present without trying to shape it into something comfortable or resolved. It can be complicated. It doesn't need to be tidy.
They were part of how I became who I am. I carry that forward.
Say it once. Go on with the day.
Written for homemade.education. The five-part ritual structure draws on the ceremony shape from MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).
